topic: Someone discovers their friend has been lying about their job
raw_output:
[story 1]

The lukewarm coffee in my mug suddenly felt like acid. I stared at the meticulously crafted LinkedIn profile Liam had shown me, the one detailing his 'innovative work in sustainable tech.' It was a masterpiece of polished jargon, and I knew, deep down, it was a fabrication. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my weight, and took a slow, deliberate sip. When Liam finally called, breathless with the details of his supposed groundbreaking project, I just nodded, my expression carefully neutral. "That sounds... intense, Liam. Really intense." I didn't press. I just let the silence hang there, heavy and expectant, watching his eyes flicker with a familiar, guilty quickness. I kept my hands folded on the desk, a quiet, immovable wall against whatever truth he was trying to hide.

[story 2]

I found the discrepancy tucked away in an old email attachment—a timestamped invoice that didn't match the narrative Liam had spun about his freelance consulting gigs. A tight knot formed in my stomach, a restless energy buzzing just beneath my skin. When we met for lunch, I deliberately kept my answers clipped and short. "So, the big launch is going well?" I asked, my voice level, almost too calm. Liam launched into a lengthy description of his 'executive role,' his words tumbling over each other like loose gravel. I just watched the way his jaw tightened when he mentioned the specific industry jargon I recognized as completely foreign to his supposed field. I picked up my napkin, smoothing out a wrinkle that wasn't there, and offered him a small, unreadable smile. He looked like he was bracing for impact, but I felt only a cool, steady resolve settling over me.

[story 3]

The realization hit me like a physical shove. It wasn't a dramatic confrontation; it was the slow, creeping certainty of a perfectly arranged lie unraveling. I spent the afternoon scrolling through archived company directories, cross-referencing the names and dates Liam had provided. The evidence stacked up, undeniable and cold. When he finally admitted the truth, his face flushed a mottled red, and he immediately tried to pivot, launching into a defense full of practiced excuses. I didn't interrupt. I simply stood up, gathering my things with slow, measured movements. "I understand," I said, my tone utterly devoid of judgment, which somehow made it sharper. "It's just... disappointing." I turned and walked out, the sound of the door clicking shut echoing the finality of my decision.
